title: A Word About the Writers' Home "Slovo"

chapters.title: The start of Slovo
chapters.text:
By the end of the 1920s, the Ukrainian SSR was riding the wave of Ukrainianization, a policy that comrade Stalin himself seemed to back. At least that is how it looked at first. During this wave, a handful of writers got the idea to set up a writers house as a cooperative. The group included Yurii Voitsehivsky, Oleksa Slisarenko, Petro Panch, Serhii Pylypenko, Ostap Vyshnia, and others. Most of them lived in cramped, stuffy communal apartments and dreamed of having a place of their own.

In February 1929, something unusual happened in Moscow: a week dedicated to Ukrainian culture. Stalin met with leading Ukrainian artists in a warm, relaxed setting and told them he supported their wish to write in their own language. Filled with hope, the writers had no idea that within four years, nearly all of them would be declared enemies of the people.

So, in 1930, a large C shaped building rose at 9 Chervonyh Pysmennykiv Street, in the Nahirnyi neighborhood. They called it Slovo. The first residents moved in that December. Life poured into the building, and it began to feel like a giant beehive: five entrances, five floors, and sixty eight roomy flats.

For its time, Slovo was a real luxury, with four or five room apartments, most of them with at least one balcony and some with two. Each apartment had its own bathroom, kitchen, and central steam heating. The most striking feature sat up in the attic: a sun parlor with a shower and a small concrete platform for sunbathing. The house was not flawless, though. Careless construction left the ventilation system poorly designed, and moths moved in, happily chewing through the residents clothes. After years of crowded communal living, with shared toilets and outdoor kitchens, Slovo felt like a little piece of heaven to its tenants.

It is worth noting that the building was not designed as a haven for the Ukrainian intelligentsia, the class of well educated thinkers. In fact, it made life easier for the NKVD, the Soviet secret police, by giving them almost endless ways to watch and control the creative Ukrainian elite. NKVD agents slipped into Slovo disguised as Soviet party critics. The party did not stop at spies, though. They also put landline phones in every apartment. A private phone in those days was a rare luxury, almost impossible to get. Of course, every one of those phones was bugged, and later, during the investigations, the prosecutors read back the residents conversations word for word.

chapters.title: The first entrance
chapters.text:
Hryhorii Epik lived in the first entrance, and you can find him listed there. He was a warm, soft spoken writer whose fate was sealed by his own novel, Soilless. Neighbors of his were shot and silenced, their work pulled from libraries. Epik suffered something worse: the regime used his name to frighten the writers who were still alive, twisting his memory into a propaganda tool.

In the summer of 1935, the party activist Pavlo Postyshev released a book that included a real letter Epik had sent to Stalin. In the letter, Epik groveled for forgiveness, admitted to terrorism and counter revolutionary activity, and urged the NKVD to destroy every Ukrainian writer. Postyshev added a note saying the artists would not be executed. Instead, they would be given a chance to undo the damage they had done to the Soviet state. The young Kulish remembered that this pamphlet wiped out any hope that the arrested slovians might return. Until then, there had been no news. After the letter, hope itself was gone.

The first entrance was also home to Mykola Khvylovy, another famous writer. Kulish recalls his fondest childhood memories as tied to this man. In his mind, the writer sits at the center of warm literary talks, gentle advice, and a copy of Works, signed in Khvylovy curving hand: To my young friend, Vova Kulish. Vova is the short form of Volodymyr. Khvylovy was the soul of the house. He loved hunting because the woods were the one place without extra ears, where he could speak without fear. He wore hunting clothes most of the time and felt uneasy in civilian dress. There is a well known VAPLITE portrait in which Khvylovy is shown in a suit with a white tie. He did not actually own a tie, so Kulish quickly folded a handkerchief into a makeshift tie to keep the official, ideologically correct look intact.

Khvylovy took his own life in May 1933, and that act is treated as the close of the Ukrainian national revival of the 1920s and 1930s. It was the moment of no return, and from then on, Slovo knew no peace.

Sosiura wrote a poem to Khvylovy with the line, you and I are crimson singers, even though they want to make us red. The crimson banner was the old Cossack standard, not the red communist one.

The first entrance also held well known Ukrainian artists, including Leonid Chernov Maloshyichenko, Mykhailo Bykovets, Pavlo Tychyna, who was later pardoned, Maik Yohansen, and Anatol Petrytsky, who was also later pardoned.

A quick note on VAPLITE, or Vilna Akademiia Proletarskoi Literatury, the Free Academy of Proletarian Literature. It was a writers group founded in Kharkiv. Its members included Mykola Khvylovy, Mykola Kulish, Hryhorii Epik, Pavlo Tychyna, and others.

The people of the house invented the word slovians as a play on slovo, the word for word. They used it as a group name for everyone living and working in the building. In Ukrainian, sloviany is also the word for the broader ethnolinguistic group known in English as the Slavs.

chapters.title: The second entrance
chapters.text:
In the second entrance lived a quiet, lovely man, the writer Valerian Pidmohylny. Volodymyr Kulish calls their friendship a cycling one, because Pidmohylny had a fine Ukraina bicycle and let him ride it whenever he asked. Even when young Kulish broke it and brought it back a week after it was fixed, Pidmohylny just smiled and said he must have been pedaling a long way. In his last years before being arrested, the famous author was banned from writing, so the only way to feed his family was to translate the great works of world literature. After his arrest, his wife and son had no choice but to move into the Kulish apartment. They lived simply but kindly, bound by the same grief, the enemies of the people being investigated at the time. After the arrest and investigation, the writer vanished, and so did his family, who spent years searching for him.

On the third floor of this entrance lived a close family friend and a literary star of the day, Ostap Vyshnia. A well known humorist, the life of every gathering and the very picture of a good mood, he knew how to crack up his neighbors until their sides ached. Pavlo Hubenko, which was Vyshnia real name, loved dogs and had as many as four. Kulish remembered most of all a giant Great Dane named Tsiatsia, about the size of a well fed calf. The dog had a sweet nature and always greeted visitors with a happy wagging tail. The unlucky ones, though, were those who got a slap from that tail, which was hard as steel wire. They were stuck with bruises that took weeks to fade. Like Khvylovy, Vyshnia was a literary friend of the young Kulish and was always lending him books from his own library.

Khvylovy suicide hit Vyshnia hard. After years of persecution and exile, Vyshnia eventually made his way back and kept working in Kyiv.

When Vyshnia heard the news of Khvylovy death, he broke down in bitter tears and cried, what have they done to you? Since the house had ears, those words quickly reached the right offices.

The second entrance also housed talented painters Ivan Padalka and Vasyl Sedliar, along with writers Oleksa Varava and Ivan Dniprovskyi.

chapters.title: The third entrance
chapters.text:
On the ground floor of the third entrance, the home of the composer Pylyp Kozytsky was rarely quiet. Music drifted out almost constantly. Children from Slovo gathered under his windows and stood listening. The apartment was a regular source of piano pieces and operatic singing. Kozytsky was a dignified, calm, soft spoken man with a pleasant manner. Volodymyr Kulish remembered that he was so unflappable that when a burglar broke in and stole his gold watch, the composer did not raise a fuss. He ran after the thief, shouting, Bastard, bastard. The passersby had no idea what was happening and did not help, so the thief escaped. There was trouble in the family in the end. The composer moved to Kyiv, his wife remarried, and she later ended up in a German camp.

Oles Dosvitnii lived with his family on the second floor. A cheerful, open, kind man, he was a devoted father and a passionate hunter with a sizable collection of rifles. He went out hunting often with Khvylovy, Yohansen, and others. In a cruel twist, that very passion for hunting and rifles helped seal his doom. Dosvitnii apartment was searched more than once for hidden weapons, and, predictably, the rifles were found right on the walls. Dosvitnii was executed as an enemy of the people. The mood was different at the apartment of Mykhailo Yalovy, who wrote under the pen name Yuliian Shpol. He was a kind neighbor and a true friend of Khvylovy, Dosvitnii, Vyshnia, and Kulish. Charged with running a terrorist group, he became the first slovian to be taken.

Khvylovy left a suicide note that read: Yalovy arrest is the execution of an entire generation. For what? For us being the most sincere communists? I do not understand anything. First of all, I, Mykola Khvylovy, am responsible for the Yalovy generation.

A floor up sat the modest Kulish family apartment, a regular meeting place for writers, always loud with jokes and laughter. On the fourth floor, you might run into the handsome, gifted, and equally proud Oleksa Slisarenko. There was a rumor at the time about a bold letter Slisarenko had written to the Russian writer Maxim Gorky, in which he called him a chauvinist and a bastard from the banks of the Volga.

Khvylovy is said to have advised: you should read the original books, and if you do not know the original language, then read them only in the Ukrainian translation.

Volodymyr Kulish

chapters.title: The fourth entrance
chapters.text:
Two close friends, Mykola Bazhan and Yurii Yanovsky, show up together on the fourth entrance list. They shared the third floor. Bazhan place looked nothing like the plainer flats around it, with plush furnishings, dark wood furniture, costly rugs, and a fine library. The young Kulish remembered that Mykola Platonovych owned an astonishing luxury for the era, a radio. He and his friend once begged to tune in to stations from abroad. The radio was not cooperating that day, so the bored boys crumpled some newspaper into a ball and started kicking it around the middle of the room. When Bazhan walked in, he joined the game instead of scolding them.

Yanovsky lived in the same grand apartment with his wife, Tamara Zhevchenko, a well known actress from the Berezil theater. They were a striking pair, and the only ones in Slovo who addressed each other as Sir and Madam. Yanovsky was crazy about photography, collected cameras, and took pictures of his friends and colleagues whenever he could. He and Bazhan were both eventually pardoned, and they all moved to Kyiv together, where they lived in constant fear and harassment.

The cold reality of the red terror and the choice between life and death frayed the bonds between once inseparable friends. Bazhan counter revolutionary activities and his steady financial support for the families of repressed artists made him a marked man. His case file kept growing, fed by new denunciations from those closest to him, and arrest felt inevitable. Bazhan was saved by chance. To mark the anniversary of Shota Rustaveli in 1937, he translated the great Georgian epic The Knight in the Panther Skin into Ukrainian. Stalin read it and awarded him the Order of Lenin, which saved his life.

The award was a lifeline, but it was also a chain. Pulled into the ranks of the official Soviet writers, Bazhan had to learn the new rules to stay alive. The costliest thing he gave up was his friendship with Yanovsky. Trapped inside the Soviet machine, he had to take part in the persecution of his friend, and he was sorry for it until his dying day.

The slovians, like many others from the Ukrainian intelligentsia, were charged under Article 58 of the Criminal Code of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic for counter revolutionary activity, treason, and espionage.

chapters.title: The fifth entrance
chapters.text:
On the second floor of the fifth entrance was the home of the great Ukrainian lyric poet Volodymyr Sosiura, the author of the famous patriotic line Love Ukraine. He was the main cupid of the Ukrainian SSR. His poems gave life to love in the 1930s, fed it, and inspired it. Romantic evenings often looked like Sosiura poetry readings, and young people scribbled down his verses and recited them to each other.

He had a complicated character, famous for endless love affairs and a complete lack of common sense. Although he earned some of the highest fees of any writer, he lived in poverty. The family slept on the floor, there was almost no furniture, and arguments and fights were a regular part of home life. He spent every ruble on fun and on friends who took advantage of him. That wild way of life led to repeated breakdowns and, in turn, to being sent to a psychiatric hospital again and again. Even though the NKVD had a conflict with Sosiura, they let him live.

The Ukrainian artists killed by the Soviet government in the 1920s and 1930s are known as the Executed Renaissance.

The last apartment, number sixty eight in the fifth entrance, belonged to the master of Ukrainian drama, an outstanding director and innovator, Les Kurbas. He gave his last breath to the Berezil Theater and spent day and night there. As a result, he usually came home either very early in the morning or late at night. Despite the shared artistic calling, Kurbas had a cold relationship with Dovzhenko, and the feeling was mutual. Mykola Kulish, the famous playwright, was his best friend. The two seemed made for each other. Kulish wrote the plays, and Kurbas brought them to life on stage, a perfect pair. Volodymyr Kulish remembered that the evenings when his father came to Berezil to read his works were a real celebration. Even though he was not an actor, the playwright embodied the characters on stage in ways even trained actors recognized, and Kurbas himself borrowed those tricks in later productions.

When Kulish was arrested, Kurbas stayed a loyal friend. None of the party suggestions could tempt him to turn his back on the enemy of the people or on Kulish controversial views. For the first time in his life, Les Kurbas dropped his usual reserve and respect, and spoke about the party leadership in blunt, vulgar terms. That moment became his own death sentence. In October 1933, the Berezil Theater, the heart of Kurbas world, was shut down. While in exile, the director kept working creatively, running the central theater of the White Sea Baltic Canal, a ship canal in Russia, near the Vedmezha Hora, or Bear Mountain, camp, where he staged plays for half a year. Stepping on a stage for the first time in a long while, Kurbas said with tears in his eyes, My God, a theater.

A striking detail: at the age of twenty six, Les Kurbas, heartbroken from unanswered love, tried to take his own life and shot himself in the chest. He somehow survived, but the bullet stayed in his heart for the rest of his days.

chapters.title: Life among the slovians
chapters.text:
Despite the dark fame Slovo carries today, at the start it was an island of creative freedom, a bright spot against the gray backdrop of Soviet Kharkiv. The residents paid close attention to fashion. When one of the slovians showed up with a new piece of clothing or brought back some unusual item from abroad, the others quickly copied it as best they could.

The Slovo house could fairly be called a dense concentration of talent per square meter.

Many of the writers loved to hunt. They liked being out in nature, where there were no extra ears, and they could talk openly. When the men came back from a trip, they brought plenty of funny stories with them. The author remembered, for example, his father coming home one day with three ducks and a snipe. That evening, he told grand stories about heroically hunting down his big game. When his wife started cleaning the birds, she found a tag that read Ukrptakhprom Kharkiv, the local poultry farm, on each duck. The playwright was not thrown off. He said the tag was a standard way of marking birds for later identification.

Sports was another shared passion. The residents built a volleyball court in the yard, where friendly neighborhood tournaments were held. It was not just for the kids. Older and younger slovians played side by side, putting age differences aside for a while. Over time, the writers of Slovo became quite good at volleyball. When theater troupes came to Kharkiv on tour, they organized large tournaments. The games usually ran until sunset, and the players headed out for a beer together afterward.

In winter, the court was flooded and turned into a skating rink, and crowds of noisy Slovo residents came out to skate. Mykola Khvylovy was a real artist on the ice. He could pull off the giant steps and other elegant figure skating moves.

chapters.title: Wrapping it up
chapters.text:
The finest of the Ukrainian creative elite, including writers, poets, playwrights, directors, set designers, composers, and painters, lived and worked in a strangely shaped building at 9 Chervonych Pysmennykiv Street in the city of Kharkiv. The story of Slovo began in December 1930 on a hopeful note, because the new building promised a fresh start and new chances for the Ukrainian intelligentsia. Three years later, that story came to a dark end, with windows that had once glowed with creative fire going dark.

The Slovo building turned into a prison, a holding cell for people sentenced to death, people who would not obey the regime and who wanted to write in Ukrainian. The red conveyor swallowed almost everyone, and the small group that survived lived out their years in fear and wandering.

A few families were pardoned, but the label of enemy of the people marked them as outsiders and made them targets for more persecution. The once cheerful, smiling kids running around the Slovo courtyard grew into wounded people living in a world without parents.

In a strange twist, Volodymyr Kulish memories of Slovo, which is now a byword for the pain, fear, and tragedy of the Ukrainian people, are filled with kindness, smiles, funny stories, and warm moments that stay with you. They form a steady backdrop to the horror that unfolded in Kharkiv in 1933. The light, the values, and the stubborn spirit of the residents were so strong that they held their warmth across the years, and they still keep little Kulish warm in the darkest times.

A few things you can try.

Think back on the history of your own country and try to remember times when creative thinkers were persecuted. Why did it happen? How did it shape your country? Try to draw international parallels between your nation story and that of Ukraine, and see what we have in common.

If you read all the way to the end, well done.
